data.title: JD.Com Is A Worthwhile Bet At This Valuation
data.description:
As you can see below, JD.com (JD) has seen a miserable past 52 weeks. The stock has fallen more than 25% and based on a quick glance at the chart, it doesn't seem it's in a hurry to recover those losses. Today, we'll take a look at their financials and recent developments to see if there is significant fundamental opportunity in JD.com at current levels. The company did report earnings quite a while ago but at the same time, they're not reporting again until early March and so I believe initiating coverage now is still appropriate.

Below, it is shown that JD.com's fundamentals are overall shaky with their margins getting weighed on by new businesses and cash flows being weak. While their push into logistics and other businesses may be a bit of a large gamble, it seems the company has adequate capital to buy time for these investments to payoff. Furthermore, with the valuation being at very depressed levels, I view the overall fundamental setup to be positive and so I'm initiating with a buy rating.

Revenue Analysis

Firstly, before we start with an analysis of their revenues, note that USD figures differ slightly platform to platform due to exchange rate discrepancies but the overall picture should be the same. For 2025 Q3, JD.com reported net revenues of RMB229.1 billion or $42 billion. This represents a growth rate of around 15% and you can see in the above chart that while this is a deceleration from Q2, this growth rate is still very respectable relative to levels we have seen in the past few years. Therefore, while consumer confidence in China is still far below 2021 levels, the company has shown that business activity has signs of improvement. Note that they were also able to beat consensus by about $860 million and so their top line was much better than expected.

While JD.com is of course product revenue dominated since it operates a large retail e-commerce platform, you can see that the strongest growth driver for the company is now services. Both on the multiyear timeframe and for Q3 YoY, net service revenue growth is outpacing net product revenue growth with the company increasing their presence in logistics. Is this increased focus on logistics the right direction for the company? The answer to that is a bit unclear in my view and we'll discuss this below.

Margin Under Pressure

Currently, JD retail is growing a bit sluggishly but their operating margin has actually expanded from 5.2% to 5.9% YoY. While product sales obviously come with relatively thin margins, this improvement is a signal that this segment is performing in a respectable manner. Here's why I'm not overly sure that the push into logistics is going to payoff in the near term.

While growth in JD Logistics greatly outpaced their JD Retail segment, you can see that operating income actually shrunk from already quite low levels. The logistics business is not a particularly easy one and is overall known to be quite capital intensive and so you can see that the profitability of this growth driver is really not there yet. As they continue to scale, it may be a different story down the road but at this point, bottom line results are getting weighed on.

As for their new businesses, revenue growth is sky high but overall contributions to the company remain low at this point. While, I'm definitely not against investments for future growth, the company does seem to be bleeding cash significantly in their new businesses. The RMB15.7 billion operating loss is a major widening YoY and almost completely offsets the operating income in Retail and Logistics and so at this point, JD.com seems to be making a pretty large gamble with their new businesses.

In my view, currently they are in a high-risk, high-reward phase since if they can make logistics and these new businesses profitable, they could be in good shape. At the end of they day, they reported a company-wide adjusted operating margin of just 0.1%, down from 5.0% YoY and their adjusted EPS saw a 56% decline to $0.53. Therefore, there are good reasons to be at least a little cautious on their strategy in my view.

Key Metrics Deteriorate

When looking at some other key metrics, there are signs of business deterioration for JD.com. For TTM inventory turnover days, there was been a considerable increase YoY to 35.8 as a sign that demand isn't really there or that there is poor inventory management. Products are essentially sitting in their warehouses longer.

For TTM accounts payable turnover days there was a slight increase YoY. With their cash balance being at RMB113 billion as of September 30th, it seems that they aren't having trouble paying bills and the increase in A/P turnover days likely just reflects better negotiated terms of payment. However, the good news ends there.

For TTM accounts receivable turnover days, there was a very meaningful increase from 5.8 to 8.3 YoY. This is a signal of weak and inefficient collections and of course that isn't a good sign for their cash flows by any means.

As you can see above, TTM FCFs have plunged by over 60% as a signal that the company's cash generation is currently weak. There was significant CAPEX in the TTM but note that TTM adjusted operating cash flows were also quite weak vs 2024 levels and so the FCF weakness isn't just because of their new business investments. Overall, I would say that these key metrics show more reasons for caution.

Recent Developments

Consumer Confidence Remains Low

While there are some signs that consumer confidence in China is starting to recover, it is a long way back to 2021 levels. Overall, for an e-commerce based company like JD.com, this shows that the near term outlook is murky right now and so while business activity may continue to bounce back, investors will likely need to be patient. With the geopolitical environment being rather unstable, I would expect a potentially bumpy recovery from here.

Some Capital Developments

In early January, there were two notable developments regarding the company's capital. It was reported that for CY2025, the company bought back around $3 billion in stock. As discussed earlier, JD.com's TTM FCF's plunged YoY and so their significant share repurchases does provide a signal that management remains confident on the company's prospects and they likely believe their stock is quite cheap currently.

Another important development is that the company is planning to raise $1.4 billion in a dim sum bond sale to lock in cheap funding. As stated earlier, JD.com's cash pile is significant as of September 30th, and so this potential offering likely isn't because of a liquidity crunch and is more of a matter of getting a good rate on capital for future use.

Valuation Is Depressed

In today's article, JD.com's fundamentals were shown to be a bit shaky. While logistics and new businesses help to power respectable top line growth, margins were under pressure and cash flows plunged. Moving forward, the slow recovery in China's consumer confidence is also not doing them any favors. However, for long term investors, I believe the valuation is simply a bit too cheap to ignore right now.

While the multiple has been lower in early to mid 2024, the 9.164 reading now still shows the stock is very cheaply valued. There has been significant multiple contraction since early 2025 and according to Seeking Alpha, JD.com's non-GAAP forward P/E ratio is now at a ~37% discount to the consumer discretionary sector as of this writing.

I would say that despite the weak profitability and undesirable macro environment right now, there is still a good chance that the company's bets on logistics and new businesses will payoff over the long run and I view the valuation to be missing out on that side of the picture. Therefore, I believe the risk/reward is net positive for investors at this time.

Takeaway

There is no question that JD.com is experiencing pain right now. What I believe the valuation fails to capture is the potential that this short term pain could lead to long term gains. While the profitability and cash flows are justified worries, the company also still has a good amount of capital and their planned bond offering shows they should have access to more funds if needed. Therefore, JD.com's new businesses have time to gain traction and that improves their odds of success. For patient investors, I view the overall risk/reward setup to be attractive based on the deep-value valuation and so I'm initiating the stock at a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Producer Price Index: Wholesale Inflation Up 0.5% In December
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest report on the Producer Price Index (PPI) shows that wholesale inflation for final demand increased by 0.5% in December. This uptick was higher than the expected 0.2% growth and follows a 0.2% increase in November. On an annual basis, the headline PPI was unchanged at 3.0% year-over-year, coming in above the forecast of 2.7%.

Core PPI, which excludes food and energy prices, was up 0.7% in December after a flat reading in November. This was higher than the expected 0.2% monthly growth. On an annual basis, core PPI heated up to 3.3% in December from 3.1% in November, coming in above the projected 2.9% reading.

Producer Price Index: Finished Goods

The BLS shifted its focus to the "final demand" PPI series in 2014, but data for these series extend only back to November 2009 for headline PPI and April 2010 for core PPI.

Since our analysis emphasizes longer-term trends, we continue to track the legacy PPI for finished goods, which the BLS still includes in its monthly updates. As a later overlay chart will illustrate, the final demand and finished goods indexes remain highly correlated.

In December, the PPI for finished goods was flat month-over-month, down from 1.0% in November. Year-over-year, headline PPI for finished goods cooled to 1.9% from 2.6% in November.

Meanwhile, core PPI for finished goods was up 0.3% on the month, up from 0.2% in November. On an annual basis, core PPI for finished goods heated up to 3.4% from 3.2% in November.

Producer Price Index (PPI) vs. Consumer Price Index (CPI)

Both PPI and CPI illustrate monthly price changes. However, as their names suggest, the Producer Price Index measures price changes from the producer perspective, whereas the Consumer Price Index measures price changes from the consumer perspective.

PPI is thought to be a leading indicator of consumer inflation because, for the most part, when producers pay more for goods and services they are likely to pass along those higher costs to the consumer.

With that being said, during the 2020 recession, producers were unable to pass along price increases, demonstrating the higher volatility of core PPI than core CPI. This relationship is further illustrated in the next chart.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Month-End Flows And U.S. Index Outlook: U.S. Stocks Fall From Warsh's Nomination
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Global markets received a shocker this morning with the announcement of the next Federal Reserve leader. Kevin Warsh has been tapped to replace Jerome Powell when his term concludes in May. The news triggered a huge reaction around markets.

Metals, historically strong in January, were hammered by a combination of post-FOMC adjustments and month-end flows. Extreme leverage and positioning do what it does best when the trend reverses, it creates a cascade of panic across all asset classes. US stocks were caught in the fray.

All major indexes are trading lower as a beta-reducing wave sweeps through the market, with the Nasdaq and Russell 2000 leading the decline. Interestingly, this weakness is isolated to the US, as global equity markets actually rose during today's session – this really confirms the impact of the Fed announcement.

As forced liquidations risk triggering further market instability, traders should prepare for rough conditions ahead. With month-end flows colliding with the weekly close and lingering anxiety over Iran, the market mood is decidedly timid as the weekend approaches – keep your eyes wide open!

Today's picture is not a pretty one, with almost everything down except consumer defensives, led by Verizon (VZ) after huge earnings and strong projections, and Tesla (TSLA), the best performer among megacaps.

Dive into our daily session charts and pre-FOMC trading levels for the major US indexes: Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Dow Jones 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The Dow Jones is now evolving within a descending channel right after testing its all-time highs – not such a worrying sign as prices remain close to the recent records, but watch out as cracks emerge.

Still, the late afternoon rebound is helping the outlook for the index which points to a more rangebound action ahead (discarding any black swan events).

Keep these two levels close for next week:

  • Above 49,100, the price action is clear and points toward a pursued run to at least a test of the all-time highs
  • Below 48,400, however, further downside is to be expected around 47,500

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Christmas ATH Daily Mom. Pivot – 49,000 to 49,100
  • Resistance 49,200 to 49,300
  • ATH resistance from 49,500 to 49,700
  • All-time highs: 49,710
  • 50,000 potential psychological resistance

Support Levels

  • Intraday pivot 48,600 to 48,700
  • 48,487 session lows
  • Key support around 47,500
  • 45,000 psychological level (main support on higher timeframe)

Nasdaq 4H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq did not get such a positive treatment from dip buyers. The tech-heavy index is testing its 25,200 mini-support. Except for a rebounding investor sentiment or more positive global news, the index looks poised for a drop towards 24,500.

Keep a close look at the Monday open – buying on Monday would help the situation, watch out what happens if dip buyers don't show up!

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Session highs: 25,840
  • Intermediate resistance 25,700 to 25,850
  • 26,246 FOMC highs
  • All-time high resistance zone 26,100 to 26,300

Support Levels

  • Mini-support 25,200 to 25,500 +/- 75 pts
  • Session lows: 25,470
  • Wednesday lows: 24,913
  • 24,500 main support
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 support

S&P 500 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 is looking more robust than its peers, particularly as it rebounded close to its session highs ahead of today's close (6,969). Similarly to the Nasdaq and Dow Jones however, it will have to breach back above its pivot zone to clear bearish factors (6,975) – rejecting the current levels could bring more consistent outflows.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Previous ATH resistance now pivot 6,945 to 6,975
  • Current ATH: 7,020
  • All-time high resistance 7,000 to 7,020 (testing)
  • Potential breakout targets (Fibonacci extensions)
    • 1.362 = 7,080
    • 1.618 = 7,119

Support Levels

  • Minor support 6,880 to 6,900
  • Mini support 6,830 to 6,850 (Greenland lows)
  • 6,800 psychological support
  • 6,789 Greenland lows
  • 6,400 major psychological support

Safe trades, and keep an eye on sentiment on Monday!

Original Post

data.title: AAON: Shares Need To Cool Down
data.description:
One company that I have known about for quite some time now, but I have not written about, is AAON, Inc. (AAON). For those not familiar with the business, it essentially operates in the HVAC (heating, ventilation, and air conditioning) solutions market. It does this not only for commercial customers but also for industrial indoor environments. Conceptually, this is a simple business to understand. But simple does not always translate to unsuccessful. In fact, management has achieved remarkable success in recent years. Revenue, profits, and cash flows have a solid history of growth. And if everything goes according to plan, we should expect that growth to continue at least in the near term.

As solid as the company is, I unfortunately cannot get behind it. Even though I love the business model and I think that business has a lot going for it, the fact of the matter is that shares look very pricey on an absolute basis. But it's not just that. They're also rather costly compared to most other similar firms. If it weren’t for the fact that the company has a great growth opportunity in place, and if it weren't for the fact that the challenges it is facing from a profitability standpoint seem to be transitory, I would certainly take a slightly bearish stance. But for now, I think that a speculative "Hold" rating is the right choice.

Shares Need To Cool Down

As I mentioned already, AAON is a player in the HVAC solutions market. But operationally speaking, there is a bit more complexity to the organization. In fact, management has organized the business into three separate operating segments. One of these is its AAON Oklahoma segment, which focuses on the production and sale of semi-custom and custom HVAC systems. It also makes certain control solutions and sells aftermarket parts to customers through retail part stores and online channels. As its name suggests, this part of the company focuses on the market in Oklahoma. But that's not all. It also has operations in Memphis, Tennessee, as well as in Parkville, Missouri. It boasts two retail locations in addition to its manufacturing facilities, and even has a research and development laboratory in operation.

On top of this, we also have the firm’s AAON Coil Products segment. Through this unit, the company produces certain semi-custom and custom HVAC systems, in addition to heating and cooling coils that are used in these systems. This firm is based out of Longview, Texas, and it also services the third operating segment, which is BASX. This is perhaps the most exciting part of the company, since it focuses on the production and sale of high-performance cooling systems. This particular piece of the pie caters to the hyperscale data center market, and it also provides can ventilation solutions for clean room environments like those used in the biopharmaceutical, semiconductor, medical, and agricultural markets. It's based out of Redmond, Oregon.

Over the last few years now, management has achieved rather remarkable growth. From 2022 through 2024, revenue for the segment jumped from $888.8 million to $1.20 billion. As revenue increased, profitability improved nicely. Net income shot up from $100.4 million to $168.6 million. We saw operating cash flow more than triple from $61.3 million to $192.5 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a near doubling from $138.9 million to $241.9 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $161.9 million to $273.5 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. As was the case with the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years, revenue for the company moved up, climbing from $902.9 million to $1.02 billion. However, on the bottom line, things became a bit more complicated. This weakness on the bottom-line came entirely from the company’s AAON Oklahoma segment. You see, revenue contracted from $664.8 million to $585.7 million.

This decline in sales was blamed by management on supply chain issues from the refrigerant transition that occurred at the start of 2025, and it was also blamed on coil supply shortages that occurred during the second quarter of the year because of the company’s ERP implementation. These ultimately slowed production of coils made by the business. And as revenue took a hit here, profitability dropped, with gross profit for the segment contracting from $246.4 million to $164.1 million. It is worth noting, as the chart above illustrates, that this segment has experienced some issues since the end of 2023. So the problems experienced in 2025 were not exactly new.

As disappointing as this was to see, other parts of the company are doing considerably better. The most noteworthy would be its AAON Coil Products segment. As the chart above illustrates, revenue and profits have skyrocketed over the last few years. And in the first nine months of 2025, the $222.7 million in sales generated by the segment far surpassed the $90.9 million reported the previous year. Management claimed that this was because of its BASX-branded products for liquid cooling data centers. This makes a lot of sense considering that AAON Coil Products caters to the other operating segments of the business. So when one part of the company does well, there is the opportunity for this part to do well also.

But this brings us to the real growth story as I see it. This happens to be the BASX segment. According to management, the non-residential HVAC market is worth about $14.5 billion. But this is a large, mature, and slow-growing space. Data centers, on the other hand, are a different story entirely.

In 2024 alone, the North American data center construction space was worth about $35 billion. About 20% of that involved cooling equipment and infrastructure. And when you focus on the specific thermal management equipment, the likes of which BASX offers, you are looking at a total addressable market of between $2.5 billion and $3 billion. Already about 83% of the revenue generated by this segment involves the data center industry. So it is well positioned to capitalize on existing opportunities.

What's really exciting here is that this space is going to continue growing. According to the company, data center installed capacity was only 4GW. But right now, we are looking at between 8GW and 12GW of construction that will occur each year, with a total pipeline of between 100GW and 150GW. Backlog for this piece of the company has exploded.

According to management, total backlog for the company shot up from $647.7 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $1.32 billion in 2025. Most of that growth came from BASX, with a surge from $408.6 million to $896.8 million. That is a monumental amount of upside.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, AAON seemed to be on track to generate net profits of around $88.6 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $208 million, and EBITDA of $196.1 million. But management is forecasting annualized sales growth over the next few years of about 12.5%. You should take the company, if everything goes according to plan, to about $1.71 billion in revenue by 2027. And with the company targeting meaningful margin improvement, we can see in the table above what my estimates are for net profits, adjusted operating cash flow, and EBITDA for the next couple of years.

But even if the company achieves these targets, it would be trading at the high end of the fair value range at best. In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean. We are looking at a firm that is still trading at varied lofty multiples way out in 2027. Now, it is worth noting that, at least right now, this is a space that trades at rather high multiples anyway. In the chart below, I compared AAON to five other similar firms. As it stands today, the company is trading comfortably above most of these other players.

Takeaway

Coming into this article, I was a bit speculative about the valuation that I saw when looking at AAON, and honestly, because of how expensive the stock is, this is not something that every investor should consider buying into. Shares are expensive, though there is some opportunity here. I wouldn't go so far as to say that taking a bearish stance is appropriate. But I do think that a speculative "Hold" rating makes sense here.

data.title: General Dynamics Looks Undervalued As Submarines And Business Jets Ramp
data.description:
General Dynamics Corporation (NYSE:GD) reported its fourth quarter and full-year results, which included pressure in the aerospace business and variable growth in other segments. The stock price has lost around 1% of its value since my last report, after a significant surge in months prior. In this report, I analyze the full year, analyze guidance and update my price target for GD stock.

General Dynamics Grows By Double Digits In 2025

General Dynamics sales increased by 10.1% to $52.6 billion, with operating earnings growing 11.7% to $5.36 billion. This indicates a 10 basis points margin step up to 10.2%. Net earnings grew 11.3% to $4.2 billion, with 13.4% growth in EPS to $15.45. Total backlog surged to $118 billion, with an additional $60.9 billion in contract potential. The $118 billion backlog covers 2.2x 2025 revenues.

The Aerospace segment grew revenues by 17% to $13.1 billion. This included 21% growth in new aircraft sales driven by the G700 and G800 and 7% growth in aircraft services sales. Operating income increased 19% to $1.75 billion, with margins expanding 30 basis points to 13.3%. This was despite some margin pressure on the G600 business jet program due to lower favorable liquidated damages settlement, fewer deliveries, higher overhead costs, and tariff impacts. Backlog grew 11% to $21.8 billion, with a book-to-bill ratio of 1.2x. Going forward, tariff pressures are expected to remain present, but margins on the G700 and G800 business jet programs should improve as the company is currently going through the learning curve, which should result in higher margins.

Combat System sales grew 2.8% to $9.25 billion, with operating profits increasing 4.3% to $1.33 billion, aided by a 20-basis point expansion in margin. Ground vehicles are not the high-growth area in defense spending; at least that's currently not the case. We do note that the backlog increased by more than $10 billion to $27.2 billion. This was driven by $10 billion in intake from Europe. So, growth is small, but the backlog supports higher revenues that are likely to materialize after 2027.

Marine Systems sales grew 17% to $16.7 billion. The growth was driven by submarine programs moving from investment phase to production phase. On long-term contracts, revenues are recognized as a percentage proportionate to the cost progress. So, as more work is being carried out now, costs are increasing and revenues are being recognized against those costs. Profits increased almost 26% to $1.18 billion, with margin expansion of 50 basis points to 7%. This reflects the transition we see in the program phases and also directly reflects the shift in the cost/revenue mix. Backlog increased from $39.8 billion last year to $52.3 billion this year, driven by orders for two Virginia-class submarines, an additional DDG-51 guided-missile destroyer and two fleet replenishment oilers.

The Technologies segment revenues increased 2.6% to $13.5 billion. Growth was limited during the year due to the government lockdown and contract scrutiny from DOGE. Margins remained stable at 9.5%, while profits increased by slightly more than 1% to $1.28 billion. The segment’s backlog increased by $2.6 billion to $16.7 billion, with a full estimated contract value of nearly $50 billion.

General Dynamics Guidance Is Realistic, But Not Strong

For the full year, consolidated revenues are guided at $54.3 billion to $54.8 billion, which is roughly $2 billion higher than last year, with margins expected to climb 20 basis points to 10.4%. Aerospace revenues are expected to increase $500 million, driven by a combination of mix and higher deliveries. However, we note that the number of deliveries has been guided up only two units higher, which is somewhat underwhelming. Margins are expected to increase to 14%, driven by learning curve effects. Combat Systems sales are expected to climb by roughly 5% with a 30-basis point headwind on margins. Marine System sales are expected to be up $0.6-$1 billion, with a 30-basis increase in margins providing a step-up in performance. Technologies sales are expected to climb $300 million, but with lower margins. Overall, the company is guiding for a 3.7% increase in sales and around 6% higher operating profits. If you look at budgets expanding fast in many parts of the world, 6% higher operating profits does not appear to be extremely strong. However, we note that it's a multi-year process to step up the revenues and profits.

General Dynamics Stock Trades At A Discount To Peers

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

We note that General Dynamics stock is currently fairly valued with 2027 earnings in mind. I believe that makes sense, given that the market should be forward-looking. However, we also note that the company is undervalued against peers, while the market dynamics and segment dynamics for General Dynamics are set to improve. The compounded annual growth rates on sales through 2027 is not really impressive, at 4.3%. Modest margin improvements will aid 7.4% annual growth in EBITDA. Where things might be a bit more disappointing is on the free cash flow metric. As capital expenditures are set to climb, the annual growth on free cash flow will be only 7%, which is below the EBITDA growth rate. I believe that the median EV/EBITDA of General Dynamics does not accurately reflect higher margin performance for the aerospace business, sales in Marine Systems ramping up, and the multi-year growth trajectory for Combat Systems. I value the stock in between the stock prices indicated for 2025 using the peer group EV/EBITDA multiple and the peer group. This brings the price target to $410.75, indicating 17% upside. So, it's a modest lift from my previous target of $407.20.

For General Dynamics, the margins are expected to climb from 12% in 2025 to 12.7% by 2027. It's not an extremely impressive combination of margin expansion and revenue growth. However, it still brings high single-digit EBITDA growth. Free cash flow performance is expected to somewhat trail on higher capital expenditures. We also see that back in the capital intensity, which is set to increase to 27% this year, and in 2027 it will be year-on-year but still elevated compared to 2023-2025. So, what we're seeing is that in order to generate growth, the company has to invest. While that may be somewhat disappointing, I do believe that the company is still set to reduce its net debt position substantially in the years ahead, even with value being returned to shareholders. So, from a net-debt leverage perspective, I believe the company is in an extremely good spot.

Conclusion: General Dynamics Is Not High Growth But Still Attractive

If you're looking for an aerospace and defense stock that's showing double-digit growth on key metrics, General Dynamics is not the name you should be investing in. However, I believe that the company is trading at a substantial discount to peers while it is seeing multi-year upside on aerospace sales, ground vehicles, and marine systems, justifying an expansion of the trading multiple, which would indicate 17% upside to the current stock price. As a result, I'm maintaining my buy rating.

data.title: Grocery Outlet May Present An Attractive Turnaround Story
data.description:
Grocery Outlet Holding Corp.'s (GO) stock has seen a broadly downward trajectory since it IPO'ed in June 2019 after a period of private equity ownership. However, it's now attractively valued with a relatively new external CEO and several catalysts for growth and potential re-rating. The business model appears robust, somewhat capital-light compared to peers, and seems to be differentiated.

Reasons to Consider Grocery Outlet

  • The company is differentiated due to its "treasure hunt" value proposition and its "Independent Operator" (IO) model, which has some (but not all) of the positive aspects of franchising. These attributes should enable superior shareholder returns over time.
  • The new CEO, Jason Potter, joined the company in Feb 2025, and he has a lot of "low-hanging fruit" ideas that are being implemented.
  • The company is now likely putting a period of restructuring costs and a drawn-out and apparently disruptive ERP implementation behind it, leading to less operational disruption and improved cash flow.
  • The recent closure of Amazon (AMZN) Fresh's somewhat competitive offering may help comps in calendar 2026 (store closures in February/March 2026)
  • The company has the ability to scale its current model with a market cap of just over $900M and 563 stores as of September 2025.
  • Currently, Grocery Outlet may be considered attractively valued: est. $260M 2025 fiscal EBITDA and 2025 est. $0.75 EPS implies 5.2x EV/EBITDA and 12.5x price to earnings.

Differentiation of Grocery Outlet

U.S. grocery is a tough market, but Grocery Outlet has two things that differentiate them. A treasure hunt experience that helps bring down costs and create a unique customer experience and an IO model that creates very strong financial incentives for store managers.

The Treasure Hunt Experience

The first is their "treasure hunt" model. This means that the Grocery Outlet procurement team is very happy to buy unusual or unwanted products at heavily discounted prices. Consider the following hypothetical scenarios:

  • A cereal manufacturer has a product fairly close to expiration and needs to move it quickly before it expires and becomes worthless.
  • An ice-cream manufacturer overproduced a certain flavor and wants to move the inventory rather than having it sitting in the warehouse.

In these cases, Grocery Outlet is the single grocery operator that is set up to move these sorts of products and that the food manufacturers can call to solve a problem. It's a win-win: the food manufacturers can move otherwise unwanted products, improving sales and capital efficiency, and Grocery Outlet can offer their customers an ongoing series of discounts on an unexpected set of items (the so-called treasure hunt experience). Obviously, these products can be acquired at low cost, supporting margins for Grocery Outlet even if the item is heavily discounted compared to customer expectations.

This is classic differentiation. It's hard for another grocery chain to do this, as it's a specialist skill, and Grocery Outlet's customers expect and are on the lookout for these "random" deals.

The Independent Operator Model

Grocery Outlet uses an "Independent Operator" (IO) approach at most stores. This is a model that's somewhat similar to franchising, but with slightly different incentives.

The IO is selected to run the store. They typically put up $350k and earn 50% of the store's gross margin. They are responsible for wage and benefit costs and local marketing. They also run the store day-to-day but leverage shared services and procurement from the Grocery Outlet organization.

An Illustrative IO Model for Grocery Outlet

Here's a rough model of how it works in percentage of sales terms (please note that this is illustrative rather than being a financial projection). I don't believe that GO necessarily sees higher margins because of the model, but I would argue the capital contribution from the IO improves capital efficiency.

Model Grocery Outlet (% of sale) Traditional Grocery (% of Sales)
Gross margin (halved as split with IO for Grocery Outlet) 15% 30%
Wages, benefits, store operating costs and local marketing costs - but not rent (paid by IO for GO) 0% 15%
Other corporate costs 10% 10%
Resulting margin 5% 5%
Capital invested per store (less for GO as IO contributes $350k) $1.65M $2M
Return on capital (higher - due to lower denominator of store buildout costs) (lower - full buildout cost paid by corporation_

IO Incentives

This is how Grocery Outlet represents the IO model in a recent investor presentation:

The model also results in an extremely incentivized store manager (IO) and an incrementally less capital-intensive business for Grocery Outlet. Again, looking at the rough model above, the IO gets 15% of sales (gross margin split), but can easily then pay 15% out on store operating and wage and benefit costs, so it's a wash for a standard operator working a "standard" 9-5 and not looking for opportunities.

But an average store is doing $7M in revenue at a 30% gross margin. If a great IO can grow sales 5% above that at the standard margin, that's +$100k; if they can improve gross margin +1%, that's +30k, and so on and so on.

Basically the IO has the opportunity to earn hundreds of thousands of dollars annually based on incremental efficiencies at the margin. It's hard for a traditional grocery store to create as aggressive incentives for store managers who just draw a salary and a more limited bonus. And of course, any incremental cash the incentives generate is split 50/50 between GO and the Independent Operator.

However, the IO model isn't quite the same as other franchising models, because the IO, even if wildly successful, typically only runs a single store; it's often a married couple doing this at a single store. Therefore, the model doesn't quite scale to the same degree other franchising models would, where great franchises can have an outsized impact by scaling across stores. However, GO is now talking about focusing on clusters of stores with future build-out, so maybe that will enable great IOs to run several stores in proximity (if so, this would be speculative and medium-term).

A (Fairly) New CEO as a Catalyst

Jason Potter joined as CEO in February 2025 with significant grocery experience. He's taking a number of actions and scaling those that succeed this list include the following (based on both his statements and analyst chatter):

  • Rounding out the in store experience so everyday needs (e.g., Heinz ketchup, selection of proteins) are always in stock, making GO a "weekly shop" destination, rather than an add-on.
  • Improved ordering systems to manage demand for fresh produce and reduce shrinkage.
  • Refining the store layout into a more coherent experience.
  • Better engagement/partnership with IOs on training and best practices.
  • Reaping the benefits of the new ERP system (even if it was delayed with excessive implementation complications).
  • Private label - today private label is a key driver for many retailers. Grocery Outlet doesn't have a real presence in private label today, and it could help margins materially.
  • Focusing on filling in stores in clusters rather than building out totally new markets (thereby getting further scale from distribution centers, etc.).
  • Improved IO recruiting processes.
  • Energy efficiency in freezer designs.

The above are just a few examples, but I think this is an effective management style. Lots of ideas and initiatives, often drawing on past experience of what works and scaling those that do. I would expect many of these to land in 2026.

The Recent Closure of Amazon Fresh Stores as a Sales Growth Catalyst

A small competitor is closing in a way that may lead to volume growth at certain Grocery Outlet locations.

Amazon experimented in grocery for several years with Amazon Fresh/Go. Of course, they own Whole Foods as a premium experience, but Amazon Fresh was classic Amazon: low cost with lots of technology features like automated checkout. As of February (March for California), Amazon Fresh will basically shut this experiment down. It was not large (~70 stores), but the sites will either close or become Whole Foods.

Now, I view Amazon Fresh as a very close competitor to Grocery Outlet because Amazon Fresh was extremely aggressive on price. Therefore, as Amazon Fresh stores close (or move to the expensive Whole Foods format), I would expect Grocery Outlet to pick up some volume as a low-cost alternative. Here's a rough model.

Number of Amazon Fresh stores closing ~70
Proportion of Amazon stores close to Grocery Outlet (mainly in CA) 35%
Proportion of spend that moves to Grocery Outlet where stores are nearby 15%
Average spend per Amazon Fresh/Go store annually (rough guess) $7M
Resulting incremental Grocery Outlet Sales in 2026 (est. per above assumptions) +$25M
Grocery Outlet run-rate sales (as denominator) $4.7B
Same store sales growth impact for GO in 2026 (est.) +0.5%

Will This Move the Needle?

Hence the Amazon Fresh closures should be incrementally positive for Grocery Outlet, with same-store sales around 1% recently (see below red chart for quarterly SSS data) and perhaps flat for Q4 on early December guidance; a +0.5% SSS uplift would be a welcome boost.

Of course, I would hope the other initiatives as listed under the CEO topic above would contribute to growth too, but the Amazon Fresh closure seems like a recent development that can really only be positive for GO as a fairly close competitor, but we must factor in that Amazon Fresh was small-scale (only 70 stores), as the above calculation does.

Capex Savings

Arguably one of the most important aspects of the thesis for GO is capex savings. As you can see from the excerpt from the most recent 2024 fiscal 10-K, the company has generated $111M to $303M of operating cash flow over the past 3 fiscal years but spent $149M to $274M on investing. Next, I'll outline why that might change in 2026 and beyond.

First, let's normalize operating cash flow to the 3-year average of $200M.

Against that, we have an average investment cash flow of $206M over the same 3-year span. But let's look at the levers there.

Investment in intangibles and licenses has perhaps been elevated due to the ERP implementation. That could normalize to $10M from $20M for a $10M saving.

In 2024, they made an acquisition for $60M, that's unlikely to recur, so it represents a $20M saving on a three-year average.

Lastly, store scale-out is slowing. In fiscal 2024, it was 67 new stores; in 2025, they are guiding to 37 net new stores and perhaps a similar number less in 2026. This could be a material saving, at perhaps $2M of investment per new store. This could lead to a capex saving of $60M across a reduction of 30 in-store openings.

Where does that leave us?

Well, operating cash flow of $200M less $116M of adjusted capex is $84M of free cashflow for a 9% free cashflow yield.

Furthermore, 37 new stores are growth capex; if we backed them out entirely to get to more of a steady-state model, that's an incremental saving of $74M.

That would imply a run rate steady-state capex of $42M. (That may be tough and aggressive when depreciation and amortization is $108M in 2024). But if we go with the $108M number as run-rate capex, that suggests FCF of $92M for a 10% free cash flow yield, that's attractive against most U.S. comps.

Room to Scale

The ability to scale is a desirable element of investments in my view. For now, it matters less as the market is clearly pessimistic on Grocery Outlet. However, if the company can show improved cash flow and same-store sales growth, as I think it may, then there's a lot of runway ahead of it. The market cap is under a billion today with just over 500 stores. For comparison, Walmart has over 4,000 U.S. stores, and many even regional operators in grocery have 1,000 stores. I don't think GO gets to those levels any time soon or ever, but the point is if the market believes the model can scale, there's a lot of runway here to grow with new stores, rather than just with incremental growth at existing stores.

Clearly, the company is dialing back a bit on growth ambitions right now given recent performance, but the capacity to scale is a useful feature of the company if the economics can be improved. Below is the store footprint as of November 2025:

Risks

Now, despite these opportunities, this is a company with slowing same-store sales, rising debt, and relatively high short interest, so let's review the risks.

Leverage

The primary risk is that excessive leverage causes a problem; here's a summary of the debt covenant from the 2024 10-K (page 56), bolding/underlining is mine:

....The 2023 Credit Agreement also contains financial performance covenants requiring us to satisfy a maximum total net leverage ratio test and a minimum interest coverage ratio test as of the last day of each fiscal quarter. The maximum total net leverage ratio test requires us to be in compliance with a Total Net Leverage Ratio no greater than 3.50 to 1.00 as of the last day of each test period ending prior to the test period ending on or about December 31, 2025, and no greater than 3.25 to 1.00 as of the last day of each test period ending thereafter, subject to certain adjustments set forth in the 2023 Credit Agreement. The minimum interest coverage ratio test requires us to be in compliance with a Consolidated Interest Coverage Ratio (as defined in the 2023 Credit Agreement) of no less than 1.75 to 1.00 as of the last day of each test period.

Below are the company's most recent calculations. For the Q3 level of net debt, adjusted EBITDA would have to fall to below $140M to trip covenants. That appears unlikely (though not impossible). For reference, the current adjusted EBITDA guide is $260M+ for fiscal 2025, and as suggested above I believe we may be close to a low-water mark there with potential improvements in 2026, should free cashflow improve that may also bring the ability to pay down debt.

All of this said, I only have the high-level summary of the loan agreements from filings and presentations, as is customary the actual agreements are not disclosed.

Competitive Dynamics From Hard Discounters Such As Aldi

There are also risks to Grocery Outlet as hard, private label discounters continue to scale out. This includes ALDI (east coast, but scaling on the west coast) and Save A Lot (east coast); however, arguably these are not new threats, with ALDI at over 2,000 stores and Save A Lot with hundreds of stores. I believe Grocery Outlet is sufficiently differentiated, but it remains a risk to long-term positioning to the extent these or similar offerings scale further into the West Coast.

Early Market Confirmation Absent

For now, Grocery Outlet's stock has traded poorly for several months, suggesting a lack of optimism on trading prospects. Of course, the value in being contrarian is going against the market. But there is some value in waiting for the stock price to show some early signs of inflecting before establishing a full position. This means missing the initial run-up in exchange for a greater chance of overall success. For now, there is no market confirmation that my thesis is correct, adding risk.

Soft Same Store Sales (With Likely Bad Q4 Results)

The other clear risk is that same-store sales have slowed. Specifically, the company saw November trading below expectations and put out an 8-K on December 3, 2025, to summarize (bolding is mine):

As of December 3, 2025,... the Company’s preliminary comparable store sales for the calendar month of November 2025 that were made in the form of Electronic Benefits Transfer ("EBT") payments declined by approximately 8.2%. The Company's preliminary comparable store sales from non-EBT transactions declined by approximately 0.5% for the calendar month of November 2025. Based on these recent sales trends, for the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025, the Company now expects comparable store sales to be approximately flat, and expects its diluted adjusted earnings per share to be at the low end of its previous forecast range of $0.21 to $0.23. For fiscal 2025, the Company now expects comparable store sales growth at the low end of the previously issued guidance range of 0.6% to 0.9% and diluted adjusted earnings per share to be at the low end of the previously issued guidance range of $0.78 to $0.80. The Company is not otherwise providing any update regarding its outlook issued on November 4, 2025.

Now, this was issued before the final few trading weeks of the calendar year 2025 (which is a 53-week year for GO), but same-store sales may be flat for the next quarter, down from around 1% in the prior two quarters. I don't think this is news to the market; since November, the stock has sold off from over $13/share to under $10. This may be something of a one-off, and so maybe this bad news creates the opportunity on a medium-term view. The CEO also discusses November trading at a conference, via a Seeking Alpha transcript here.

Conclusion

Grocery Outlet is not without risk; if trading does not improve by 2026, the stock could be further de-rated and may even trip debt covenants. Just because the stock may seem cheap today does not mean it can't fall further.

However, this appears to be a sound and differentiated business; the treasure hunt experience is unique and hard to replicate, and the IO model reduces capital intensity and dramatically boosts store-level incentives.

The new CEO has lots of concepts for improving momentum, based on extensive experience, and arguably headwinds such as the November government shutdown, the less than successful ERP implementation, and the capital spend of elevated store openings are all rolling off, offering incremental cash flow in 2026.

The company's calendar 2026 numbers may also see a boost from Amazon Fresh store closures. That's not going to be transformative, but it's enough of a tailwind that it may matter in the context of what might be seen as a bombed-out valuation.

The market may be excessively skeptical and risk averse, and it won't happen overnight, but during 2026, Grocery Outlet may be able to show improving margins, cash flow, and same-store sales in a way that enables the business to re-rate.

For example, the average profitable grocery retail trades at around 9x EV/EBITDA today, according to NYU Stern figures. That would suggest a per-share valuation of a little over $17/share for the stock (~94% upside), which is around where it was trading in August 2025 for reference.

Furthermore, that's only for the "average" grocery stock. I believe Grocery Outlet may deserve to trade at something of a premium given its superior business model. For example, I would definitely not be a buyer of Grocery Outlet at 15x EV/EBITDA, but that would be a $32 share price as an example of what could happen if the market became exuberant about GO.

Potential Catalysts With Timing (Illustrative)

  • Q4 figures are bad, but not catastrophic, with positive early hints from Jan/Feb-late February 2025.
  • Q1 figures show some incremental progress and positive guidance - early May 2026.
  • Q2 starts to show that the weakest results are behind the company - August 2026.
  • Late 2026 - improving results start to cause investors to consider GO's long-term differentiated business model rather than worry about the specific risks of the next quarter.

What Would Suggest the Thesis is Wrong

If same-store sales in H1 are negative after the "exceptional" (and somewhat expected) shutdown-related weakness in Q4, then I'd have to consider if the business is able to compete against hard discounters such as ALDI scaling up and if the treasure hunt model has value. I believe the market is currently overreacting to a combination of operational short-term issues, but I could be missing more long-term structural threats.

Also, I believe management can show strong cash flow in fiscal 2026 even if same-store sales are positive but soft. However, if management wants to pursue lots of capital-intensive growth initiatives such that the debt load rises in 2026, then I'd likely reconsider my investment in the company as a potential covenant breach draws perhaps closer. I'd like 2026 to be a year when they highlight the cash flow potential of the business and create a little more headroom on the debt load.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Top 2026 Long Idea investment competition, which runs through February 28. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: S&P 500 Earnings: 7,000 Tough Resistance For S&P 500
data.description:
Silver had an ugly day on Friday, January 30th. 500 mln shares were traded versus an average of 78 mln, and the iShares Silver Trust (SLV) was down $30 or 28.6% on the day. The basic materials sector, of which silver, gold, copper and alumina companies are a part, is just 2% of the S&P 500 by market cap.

So what’s happening with S&P 500 earnings?

This spreadsheet layout (data from LSEG I/B/E/S) shows the steady progression of expected EPS growth rates, but also note expected revenue growth rates.

Expected EPS growth by sector:

Looking at both 2025 and 2026, note the big change in basic materials EPS growth in 2026, as noted a week or two ago.

Note how the energy sector’s revisions for ’26 have been seeing downward pressure since late last summer. The sector is actually trading pretty well, and some of the charts show breakouts in the sector. Do you watch the price or the earnings trends?

Financials have been seeing lower revisions too for ’26, possibly thanks to the interest rate cap? Industrials, too, are seeing slowing growth rates for 2026. This could change with first quarter reports that will start in April ’26.

Note too how the “expected” 2026 S&P 500 EPS growth of 15.1% today was 15.5%–15.6% in late December ’25, early ’26. There has been some modest downward pressure on estimates after an unusually strong 6 to 9 month period.

Summary/conclusion

This blog has a ton of individual equity earnings previews or summaries (after-earnings look) to get to, so the weekly earnings update is just to show readers what the numbers say.

Here’s one last data point:

Expand the spreadsheet above, which shows weekly closes for the S&P 500 since late October ’25. The S&P 500 is having trouble punching through 7,000. (The actual S&P 500 close on 1/30/26 was 6,939.)

Also, Microsoft’s (MSFT) drop on Thursday, January 29th, can’t be ignored. It was the worst day for the stock since the March 2000 Covid drop.

Looking at the expected EPS growth by sector above, 2026 technology sector EPS expected growth estimates continue to see higher expected EPS growth for the first few weeks of 2026. We’re in a gray area right now between estimates and price action.

None of this is advice or a recommendation but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. All S&P 500 earnings data is typically sourced from LSEG I/B/E/S. None of the above information may be updated, and if updated, may not be done in a timely fashion. Readers and investors should determine their own comfort with portfolio volatility and adjust accordingly.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: LyondellBasell's Crucial Moment: Will The Board Protect The Dividend?
data.description:
After giving LyondellBasell Industries N.V. (LYB) a Sell rating in October, I think it’s a good time to revisit the company’s outlook following the earnings report released this morning.

Q4 Results

Q4 non‑GAAP EPS came in at –$0.26, missing the $0.25 consensus estimate by a wide margin of $0.51. Revenue was reported at $7.09 billion, down 25.4% year over year, but still beating expectations by $270 million.

Focusing on the weakening margins right away highlights the big challenges the company is dealing with in this tough market environment. Management repeatedly emphasized underlying issues during the earnings call, with CEO Peter Vanacker noting that this is the most severe cycle he has encountered in his career to date.

The way he expressed himself on the earnings release - “...during an exceptionally challenging environment...” in my view, summarizes the situation exceptionally well.

Oxyfuels and Europe PE are currently the only segments performing close to their historical average margins, as shown in the table below from the earnings presentation, while other segments are falling well short of their past figures.

The fourth quarter demonstrated weakness across nearly all areas and segments, with the exception of the technology division, which licenses technology and receives royalties and therefore is not subject to the same operational challenges as other segments. All operational segments reported losses when excluding one-time costs.

The results were especially concerning when considering weakening EBITDA trends. In the most recent quarter, adjusted EBITDA—excluding certain items—was $417 million, representing a decline of over 30% compared to the same period last year. Annualizing these figures suggests EBITDA could fall even below $2 billion if that trend continues, potentially ranging from $1.6 billion to $2 billion if performance does not improve in 2026.

Management noted during the conference call that pricing pressures from China persist due to an oversupplied Chinese market and ongoing efforts by Chinese producers to increase exports. However, they remain cautiously optimistic and anticipate potential signs of rationalization in the future.

Capital Allocation

An analysis of capital allocation indicates that the company's cash balance has remained unchanged primarily due to the issuance of $1.5 billion in bonds. In summary, the company financed its dividend payments and share repurchases largely by incurring additional debt:

Dividends Were Discussed During the Conference Call

During the conference call, CEO Peter Vanacker did not reaffirm as usual the company's commitment to dividends, instead emphasizing the importance of maintaining a strong balance sheet and investment grade rating. He stated that the board is currently reevaluating the dividend and will discuss it at their next meeting in February. After his comments, I observed the share price weaken in real time, likely due to the lack of clear dividend reassurance.

Outlook

Management was most optimistic about the North America segment, where seasonal improvement is anticipated, while other segments are expected to remain stable at best.

During the earnings release and conference call, phrases like "pressure" and "soft demand" came up frequently, in my view highlighting well the ongoing challenges the company is dealing with.

The company didn't share a revenue and profit forecast for 2026, offering only limited information on capital spending, interest expenses, and depreciation and amortization costs.

...and offered some broader insights on market dynamics and operating rates:

Entering the first quarter, the company is managing continued volatility in feedstock and energy prices. In North America, tight year-end inventories, reduced supply due to winter storm Fern and stronger seasonal demand are supportive for polyethylene price increase initiatives in the market. In Europe, typical seasonal trends should lead to improved demand as the quarter unfolds. Oxyfuel profitability is expected to normalize following a volatile 2025 with typical seasonal margin improvements toward the end of the first quarter. LYB is aligning first quarter operating rates with global demand and plans to operate Olefins & Polyolefins Americas assets at approximately 85%, Olefins & Polyolefins EAI assets at approximately 75%, and Intermediates & Derivatives assets at approximately 85%.

Cash Flow, Debt, and Balance Sheet

Although the net debt-to-EBITDA ratio is still under 4, it currently sits at 3.7—satisfying creditors and supporting investment-grade ratings—the financial situation is deteriorating, and without excluding one-time costs, this would have become pretty worrisome.

As previously indicated, their free cash flow does not cover dividend payments, which have lately been financed through the issuance of new debt. Although the fourth quarter free cash flow showed clear improvement over the prior quarter, the annualized free cash flow stayed at about $500 million, falling well short of the roughly $1.9 billion paid out in dividends.

The balance sheet shows a noticeable boost in cash compared to Q3. However, as previously discussed, this is primarily due to the company's issuance of $1.5 billion in new debt, which is reflected in the long-term debt section, rising from $10.5 billion to $12.1 billion year over year.

Valuation

Using the same EV/EBITDA approach from my previous piece in October, I came up with the following results:

Share Price based on EV/EBITDA calculation, billions $

conservative

EV/Ebitda 7

conservative

EV/Ebitda 7

optimistic

EV/Ebitda10

optimistic

EV/Ebitda10
EBITDA 2 3 2 3
Cash 3.4 3.4 3.4 3.4
Debt 12.3 12.3 12.3 12.3
Net Debt 8.9 8.9 8.9 8.9
EV/EBITDA 7 14 21
EV/EBITDA 10 20 30
Equity Value 5.1 12.1 11.1 21.1
Stock count 0.322 0.322 0.322 0.322
Stock Price, Various Scenarios 15.84 37.58 34.47 65.53

Author's Creation.

This time, I modeled four different scenarios. The first two columns show the stock price assuming an EV/EBITDA multiple of 7, which is a typical conservative approach for a company operating in a highly cyclical sector, in my experience. To offer a more optimistic perspective as well, the other two columns show scenarios where the EV/EBITDA multiple is 10, one with EBITDA at $2 billion and the other with EBITDA at $3 billion. As shown in bold, the results vary quite meaningfully. I still think it makes sense to lean toward a mid-range scenario, with the fair share price settling at a reasonable value in the mid-30s for now.

By the relative valuation approach, LYB seems slightly overvalued compared to its 5-year average, but roughly in line with the sector median:

Risks

The company faces a significant feedstock risk: the AI boom is increasing energy demand, which has driven up natural gas prices (NG1:COM). This is crucial, as natural gas is the main pillar of LYB's profitability and competitiveness.

There’s also a considerable political risk: if Chinese authorities view the sector as strategically important and choose to maintain or expand their market share, they could drive prices down over the long term, regardless of economic rationale.

The execution risk of navigating the company through the ongoing, serious downcycle and the length and depth of that cycle is also a significant challenge.

The macro and tariff risks are certainly worth highlighting.

The dividend is at risk. While maintaining the dividend is certainly very important from a shareholder-friendly approach and long-standing traditional standpoint, from the perspective of the company's long-term financial sustainability, it might be better to step back on that front until the business cycle shows clear signs of improvement.

Takeaway

In my opinion, the LYB is still caught in the midst of a really challenging downturn, with no clear end in sight yet. The situation appears to be deteriorating further, as management’s tone has also become more subdued, and there are no bold expectations of significant broad improvement in the near term. The company seems focused on boosting efficiency while keeping its finances sustainable.

Although natural gas prices have previously boosted the company's above-average profitability in the North America region, recent price dynamics don’t provide that advantage at the moment. Furthermore, increased electricity demand from AI and expanding LNG exports seem to be pushing natural gas prices higher over the long term.

Management’s hesitation to directly answer questions about dividends points to a fair amount of uncertainty in that regard, in my view. The decision has been pushed to the board’s next meeting in February, but with finances weakening and the discussion being cautious, I’m not feeling confident about their chances of keeping such a high yield.

In my view, it’s better to stay patient and cautious with LYB until there are clear signs that the cycle is beginning to improve. I don’t see such indications yet.

data.title: Unit Corporation: Cash Position Still Significant After No Special Dividend In 2025
data.description:
Unit Corporation (UNTC) did not declare a special dividend in late 2025 after selling its drilling division. This was a disappointment to some investors, but it does make Unit's current $1.25 per share quarterly dividend sustainable for a longer period of time.

I have updated my valuation estimates for Unit based on end of 2026 projections now. I estimate its end of 2026 value at $29.00 to $33.85 per share if the warrants are not adjusted. The downside scenario would be $26.05 to $30.15 per share if the warrants are fully adjusted for all dividends.

I remain neutral on Unit at its current share price. It does offer a nice 15% yield currently, but may see a bit of a share price decline depending on what happens with the warrants.

Special Dividend And Interest Income

Unit held off on declaring a special dividend after the sale of its drilling division. I had thought there was a decent chance of a special dividend, but that depended on whether or not Unit wanted to maintain a larger cash position to help support its regular dividend for a longer period of time.

The decision not to issue a special dividend leaves Unit with a significant amount of cash on hand. I have been projecting Unit's year-end 2025 cash balance at approximately $180 million, including the proceeds from the sale of its drilling division.

This would translate into approximately $7 million per year in interest income at current rates. Short-term investment rates (such as the one-month T-bill rate) are now around 3.7%, down from around 4.3% to 4.4% in mid-2025. This has a slight effect on Unit's interest income, with a 0.5% to 0.6% change equating to a $1 million difference in annual interest income with its projected year-end 2025 cash balance.

Notes On Hedges

Unit had a fair amount of Q4 2025 natural gas hedges, but its hedges for 2026 are relatively modest.

Unit is approximately 15% hedged on natural gas and 18% hedged on oil in 2026, assuming that it keeps production roughly flat compared to Q3 2025 levels.

This gives Unit significant exposure to fluctuating commodity prices. At current strip prices, Unit's 2026 hedges have roughly neutral value. Unit's natural gas hedges have slightly negative value, while its oil hedges have slightly positive value.

Updated 2026 Outlook

Since I last looked at Unit, commodity strip prices for 2026 have improved somewhat. The current 2026 strip is now around $63 to $64 for WTI oil (CL1:COM) and around $4.60 for NYMEX natural gas (NG1:COM).

Natural gas prices have soared due to the winter storms, with February's contract closing at $7.46. The effect is smaller on later contracts, with the March to June 2026 contracts averaging slightly over $4.

The overall effect of the winter storms on Unit's projected 2026 free cash flow is to improve it by a bit over $10 million compared to mid-January strip prices, but the improvement is only a few million compared to when I looked at Unit in November 2025 (since natural gas futures were relatively strong then).

I am continuing to model Unit's 2026 production at roughly Q3 2025 levels, although this will depend on Unit's participation in non-operated activity.

At current strip prices, I now project Unit to generate $115 million in revenues.

Type Barrels/Mcf Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 800,000 $61.50 $49
NGLs 1,000,000 $16.25 $16
Natural Gas 12,000,000 $3.70 $44
Interest Income $7
Hedge Value $0
Total $115

This results in a projection of $36 million in free cash flow in 2026, assuming $20 million in capex to hold production roughly flat. Unit's cash G&A post-drilling divestiture remains an unknown, although I've modeled that at $14 million per year.

$ Million
Oil and Gas Operating Costs $45
Cash G&A $14
Capital Expenditures $20
Total $79

This would result in roughly 0.7x dividend coverage at a $1.25 per share quarterly dividend. Unit's projected cash balance would decrease by $13 million to $167 million at the end of 2026 in this scenario.

Unit would have approximately 1.0x dividend coverage (at $1.25 per share per quarter) at around $75 WTI oil and $4.50 NYMEX natural gas (without hedges). This also assumes $7 million per year in interest income.

Notes On Valuation

I have updated my valuation estimates for Unit for the end of 2026 projections now. I have assumed that Unit will end 2026 with $167 million in cash.

I have also assumed that it mostly replenishes its PD reserves during 2026, resulting in an upstream value of $120 million to $168 million at the end of 2026.

This would result in an estimated value of $29.00 to $33.85 per share at the end of 2026 for Unit, based on 9.9 million outstanding shares. This assumes no impact from the warrant litigation.

A scenario where the warrants end up being fully adjusted for all dividends would reduce Unit's estimated value to $26.05 to $30.15 per share. Unit's warrants would have an exercise price of $9.24 at the end of 2026 in this scenario.

A scenario where the warrants were adjusted only for the $32 per share in special dividends paid out would result in minimal impact to Unit's estimated value. In that scenario, I'd estimate a $29.00 to $33.50 per share valuation for Unit.

Unit's warrants could be worth approximately $17 to $21 at the end of 2026 if they are fully adjusted for all dividends, but would probably only be worth a couple of dollars if the adjustment was only for special dividends and nothing if there ended up being no adjustments.

Conclusion

Unit has a significant amount of cash on hand now, which boosts its interest income. After selling its drilling division, its dividend coverage is likely to be below 1.0x, but it has enough cash on hand to support its current $1.25 per share dividend for quite a long time.

I currently project Unit to end up with $13 million in cash burn after dividends in 2026, which would reduce its cash position by around 7%. I am neutral on Unit at its current share price of $33, although it may have attractiveness to investors prioritizing dividend income (it should be noted that the tax treatment of its dividends has varied over time).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Momentum Regime Is Changing - And It Favors XSMO Over SPMO
data.description:
In June 2025, I had described the Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO) as a tactical momentum vehicle rather than a buy-and-forget allocation. The core of the thesis was that SPMO excels in specific market regimes. It needs narrow leadership to lead a momentum charge and persistent trends to work out well. SPMO thrives when leadership trends for months at a time but struggles to deliver alpha over the broad market index (SPY) during consolidation, dispersions, and rotations/reversals.

In June, I had felt that the strong market for narrow leadership in large caps, where momentum did matter across the board, had largely played out. What I expected ahead was slowing momentum and dispersion within the large- and mega-cap universes. The momentum continued to remain strong for a while, but dispersion started to creep in. For a heavily concentrated style like SPMO, that often means it relies on being on the right side of concentration in such an environment of dispersion. Since my June Hold call on SPMO, SPY is up by ~16% while SPMO has delivered only ~11% in total return terms. So that June call has played out as expected.

Interestingly, while dispersion is not a necessary structural tailwind for the mega-cap dominated large cap universe, it remains so for the small cap universe for several reasons. One, dispersion here is generally higher. There are stocks that are riding momentum even when broader, smaller cap indices are consolidating. Stock selection on the basis of momentum matters more in the small cap universe always and adds the potential to generate alpha in the long run. That is where momentum strategies now start rewarding the Invesco S&P SmallCap Momentum ETF (XSMO), where dispersion is a relatively more persistent theme and concentration is more balanced to be able to generate momentum tailwinds even if the markets are not rallying at the index level. Interestingly, XSMO is also almost as close to a buy and forget allocation as can be within the momentum ETF universe, where SPMO is often a tactical bet.

Consistent Edge in XSMO - Performance Data

XSMO has delivered persistent long term alpha vs. IWM. This is seen in and around the COVID crash and subsequent rebound and through the 2022 weakness (a regime when several momentum plays generally are expected to give back alpha over their broader parent). This ability to hold on to alpha in weaker markets does not rob XSMO of the ability to grab alpha in rallies (like that seen in 2024-25).

XSMO's sustained alpha generation is in contrast with SPMO, where SPMO needs conducive momentum-driven bull legs to outperform. It gives back much of its gains in momentum crashes or even sideways markets. The alpha generation abilities are often overlooked when we compare total returns of XSMO versus SPMO. SPMO is far ahead in total return terms but requires more work from the broader market than its own alpha generating abilities. That matters when supportive regimes turn.

XSMO's Structural Edge

XSMO's consistent alpha generation - a result of holding back gains when the broader small cap index is not showing any momentum of its own and amplifying it when momentum takes center stage - is not a result of any methodological edge as such.

Both SPMO and XSMO use nearly identical definitions of momentum—12-month price return excluding the most recent month, adjusted for volatility to favor more stable trends. Stocks are ranked by a risk-adjusted momentum score, weighted by a blend of market cap and momentum score, and rebalanced semi-annually.

The structural edge comes from the universe both ETFs apply these rules to. SPMO selects ~100 stocks from a universe of 500 stocks (the S&P 500). These stocks are heavily owned. Price moves are more macro- and sector driven and correlation (even during a phase of dispersion) is usually higher. Market caps here tend to skew toward mega caps in a steeper way, and concentration builds up. XSMO, on the other hand, draws from a slightly larger universe of the S&P SmallCap 600 (and a far larger universe of small caps by number overall - say 2000 in the case of the Russell 2000 small cap index). Here, stock selection usually has material significance because of the larger universe, plus greater performance dispersion anyway due to lower analyst consensus and crowding, wider earnings variability, and greater stock specific price discovery (often greater but more volatile momentum too). The market cap dominance does not show up at the same level of concentration as it does in SPMO too. The top 10 holdings here account for only ~26% of the portfolio compared to ~51% concentration in the case of SPMO.

This diversification is also visible in sectoral allocations. Unlike SPMO, where nearly ~70% of exposure sits in three mega-cap-driven sectors (tech, communications, and financials), XSMO’s leadership is spread across cyclicals, industrials, and smaller growth segments, with no single sector dominating the portfolio. The exact sectoral allocation matters for total returns (such as higher tech allocations in SPMO), but not for alpha generation. That assessment is important for an SPY versus IWM thesis, not for momentum investors.

What matters for a momentum strategy generating alpha over the broader index is how well diversified its exposure is. That indicates whether it is deriving performance from a dominant theme or relying on company specific momentum and growth (not broad macro flows into a specific sector). XSMO's sectoral allocation is more incidental to stock selection. In the case of SPMO, right now it seems to be the opposite case.

The overall implications of the portfolio structure, methodology, and the universes the ETFs cater to are that SPMO requires its concentrated bets to go right. That happens when we have a 2024-25 like market where the momentum is stronger in the mega cap narrow leadership. Any dispersion in the mega cap bucket implies a drag. XSMO does not require any concentrated theme to play out. It relies on stock-specific momentum to work out across the portfolio. It can do well even if small caps are not trending up as a group. Dispersion is not a drag for XSMO.

XSMO for All Regimes, Especially Now

All in all, SPMO remains a Hold because it is expected to underperform SPY in the next few quarters until broader growth returns in the mega cap pack. Even in comparison with XSMO, its long term thesis is not superior anyway in terms of thematic purity - that is, momentum as a source of consistent alpha. And that is a structural issue rather than something governed by short term dynamics. For the same structural superiority, XSMO is a better long-term Buy for momentum investors.

In total return terms, SPMO benefits from its concentration in favorable conditions - the once-in-a-decade boost it got in 2024-25. That is not usual performance across cycles. In fact, XSMO's outperformance is already visible as SPMO's specific concentrated structure has started to lose its edge recently. The comparison between SPMO and XSMO is not only about total returns, though. If the large cap base is helping SPMO, then SPY remains a better bet. The same cannot be said of XSMO, where risk-adjusted returns are clearly superior to the broader underlying small cap trends.

Looking ahead, let us understand why XSMO is better placed than SPMO in the short term too. On the surface, large caps look better placed than small caps because the latter need a phase of economic expansion, lower rates, and higher liquidity to flourish. That is not on the horizon, not in the next few months at least. So, the small caps' relative underperformance of over a decade is unlikely to reverse soon, and that being a support for XSMO is ruled out. Additionally, as momentum slows, I feel investors will seek refuge in cash backed large and mega caps over narratives alone or smaller caps. That phenomenon has already been in progress over the past few quarters or so, when several narrative stock valuations have been challenged. Mega cap tech hasn't really taken off, but either remains defensive or is still moving up, although slower than what we saw in 2024-25.

Despite the relatively bullish view on large caps, SPMO's thesis suffers. This is because SPMO needs a lot more than relative resilience. It needs strong momentum, and that too without dispersion in the narrow leadership it bets on. XSMO does not need strong momentum at the broader small cap index level. It simply needs a market that is not under pressure. As long as there is scope for a few stocks' earnings to expand, XSMO will actually use dispersion to its benefit and ride selective momentum better - both because of its lack of concentration drag and a universe where momentum is usually stronger as a theme (although admittedly volatile, some stocks can go through strong trends due to lack of crowding). Today's environment is therefore conducive to outperformance in XSMO, without relying on small caps mean reverting after years of underperformance. That bounce back may come eventually too for longer term investors but is an added support, not a requirement for today's thesis.
